items.question: What is an outlier, and how do IQR and z-score detection differ?
items.answer: An outlier is an observation unusually far from the bulk of a distribution. IQR fences use Q1, Q3, and IQR = Q3 − Q1, flagging values outside Q1 − 1.5×IQR or Q3 + 1.5×IQR; quartiles are robust to moderate extremes. Z-scores use z = (x − μ)/σ and typically |z| > 3; they are easy under normality but mean and std are not robust—outliers can shift μ and σ and hide themselves.

items.question: When would you winsorize instead of dropping outlier rows?
items.answer: Winsorize when rows are scarce, labels are costly to lose, or extremes may be valid but should not dominate—e.g. cap revenue at train 99th percentile. Dropping is better when values are confirmed errors (typos, sensor dropouts) and enough data remains.

items.question: How do outliers interact with MSE and linear regression?
items.answer: OLS minimizes mean squared error, which squares large residuals. A few high-leverage points with extreme x or y pull the line to reduce those errors most. Mitigations: remove/clip bad points, transform skewed features, or use robust regression (Huber, RANSAC) that down-weights large residuals.

items.question: What pipeline mistake causes leakage in outlier preprocessing?
items.answer: Fitting quartiles, means, standard deviations, or percentile clip bounds on the full dataset (including validation/test) before splitting. Correct pattern: compute thresholds on training only, persist them in the pipeline, and apply the same numbers at validation, test, and inference.
